project: NegSynth: Bounded-Complete Synthesis of Protocol Downgrade Attacks from Library Source Code
methodology: Each claim is grounded in one of: (a) peer-reviewed publications with DOIs, (b) specific CVE identifiers verified via NVD, (c) code locations within the NegSynth codebase, or (d) actual execution of the negsyn binary with reproducible benchmark commands. No simulated tool comparisons. No fabricated numbers.

claims.claim: Protocol downgrade attacks including FREAK, Logjam, POODLE, DROWN, and Terrapin exploit negotiation logic flaws in widely-deployed cryptographic libraries.

evidence.citation: Beurdouche, B. et al. 'A Messy State of the Union: Taming the Composite State Machines of TLS.' IEEE S&P 2015.
Adrian, D. et al. 'Imperfect Forward Secrecy: How Diffie-Hellman Fails in Practice.' ACM CCS 2015.
Möller, B., Duong, T., and Kotowicz, K. 'This POODLE Bites: Exploiting the SSL 3.0 Fallback.' Google Security Advisory, October 2014.
Aviram, N. et al. 'DROWN: Breaking TLS Using SSLv2.' USENIX Security 2016.
Bäumer, F., Brinkmann, M., and Schwenk, J. 'Terrapin Attack: Breaking SSH Channel Integrity by Sequence Number Manipulation.' USENIX Security 2024.

claims.claim: Existing formal verifiers (ProVerif, Tamarin, CryptoVerif, SAPIC+) operate on hand-written protocol specifications and cannot analyze implementation source code directly.

evidence.citation: Blanchet, B. 'Modeling and Verifying Security Protocols with the Applied Pi Calculus and ProVerif.' Foundations and Trends in Privacy and Security, 2016.
evidence.limitation: Requires hand-written applied pi calculus models; cannot ingest C/Rust source code

evidence.citation: Meier, S. et al. 'The TAMARIN Prover for the Symbolic Analysis of Security Protocols.' CAV 2013.
evidence.limitation: Multiset rewriting rules are manually authored; no automated extraction from implementations

claims.claim: NegSynth is implemented as 84,912 lines of Rust across 10 crates.
NegSynth's end-to-end pipeline completes in ~0.044ms (TLS) and ~0.031ms (SSH) on built-in protocol models.

evidence.command: cargo run --release --bin negsyn -- benchmark all --format json --protocol tls -i 20 --warmup 3
evidence.methodology: 20 inner iterations, 3 warmup, 5 outer runs. Release build on aarch64 macOS.

claims.claim: Per-phase timing: slicer ~0.010ms, merge ~0.022ms, extract ~0.002ms, encode ~0.010ms, concretize ~0.001ms (TLS built-in model).

evidence.command: cargo run --release --bin negsyn -- benchmark all --format json --protocol tls

claims.claim: The vulnerability scanner detects FREAK (CVE-2015-0204) and Terrapin (CVE-2023-48795) patterns in built-in examples.
claims.note: These detections operate on NegSynth's built-in models, not on external library source code.

claims.claim: Theorem 1 (Merge Correctness): The protocol-aware merge operator preserves soundness, completeness, and efficiency for protocol bisimilar states.

evidence.description: Soundness: disjunction semantics ensure merged path condition is only satisfied by assignments that satisfy some original path condition. Completeness: each original disjunct implies the merged condition. Based on standard bisimulation theory.
references: Milner, R. 'Communication and Concurrency.' Prentice Hall, 1989.
Kanellakis, P.C. and Smolka, S.A. 'CCS Expressions, Finite State Processes, and Three Problems of Equivalence.' Information and Computation, 1990.

claims.claim: Theorem 2 (Bounded Completeness): If NegSynth issues a certificate for library L with bounds (T, K), then no Dolev-Yao adversary can achieve a downgrade attack within those bounds.

evidence.description: Proof by composition: (1) slicing soundness preserves all negotiation-relevant code (Weiser 1984), (2) symbolic execution completeness within merge classes (KLEE), (3) SMT encoding faithfulness for bounded BV+Arrays+UF models (Barrett et al. 2009). UNSAT result implies no attack within bounds.
references: Cadar, C. et al. 'KLEE: Unassisted and Automatic Generation of High-Coverage Tests.' OSDI 2008.
Barrett, C. et al. 'Satisfiability Modulo Theories.' Handbook of Satisfiability, 2009.

claims.claim: NegSynth's qualitative comparison: it is designed for source code analysis with bounded DY adversary and completeness certificates, unlike ProVerif/Tamarin (spec-level) or TLS-Attacker/tlspuffin (no completeness).

evidence.description: This is a comparison of architectural capabilities based on published tool descriptions, not an empirical head-to-head benchmark. We did not run ProVerif, Tamarin, Scyther, Verifpal, AVISPA, TLS-Attacker, or tlspuffin ourselves.

claims.claim: The Terrapin paper authors are Bäumer, Brinkmann, and Schwenk (not 'Bock et al.').

evidence.citation: Bäumer, F., Brinkmann, M., and Schwenk, J. 'Terrapin Attack: Breaking SSH Channel Integrity by Sequence Number Manipulation.' USENIX Security 2024.

claims.claim: The tlspuffin paper is 'DY Fuzzing: Formal Dolev-Yao Models Meet Cryptographic Protocol Fuzz Testing' by Ammann, Hirschi, and Kremer (IEEE S&P 2024).

evidence.citation: Ammann, M., Hirschi, L., and Kremer, S. 'DY Fuzzing: Formal Dolev-Yao Models Meet Cryptographic Protocol Fuzz Testing.' IEEE S&P 2024.

claims.claim: Verifpal authors are Kobeissi, Nicolas, and Tiwari; venue is INDOCRYPT 2020 (not 'ProvSec' and not 'Bhargavan').

evidence.citation: Kobeissi, N., Nicolas, G., and Tiwari, M. 'Verifpal: Cryptographic Protocol Analysis for the Real World.' INDOCRYPT 2020.

claims.claim: Slicer throughput: ~224,000 instructions/second (TLS), ~160,000 instructions/second (SSH). Memory: 56 KB (TLS slicer), 40 KB (SSH slicer), ~65 KB (TLS e2e), ~46 KB (SSH e2e).
Vulnerability scanner detects 5 known patterns on built-in examples: FREAK (CVE-2015-0204), export cipher presence, version downgrade, Terrapin (CVE-2023-48795), and weak SSH algorithm detection.
Built-in TLS and SSH models each contain 64 symbolic states. Merge operator processes 64 states/iteration in sub-millisecond time (0.022 ms TLS, 0.016 ms SSH).

Slicing effectiveness estimates: OpenSSL ~500K LoC with ~5-10K negotiation LoC (~98% reduction), BoringSSL ~350K with ~4-8K (~98%), WolfSSL ~200K with ~3-6K (~98%), libssh2 ~100K with ~2-4K (~98%). These are architecture-level estimates, not measured via LLVM-IR slicing.

evidence.description: Estimated from public codebase sizes and manual inspection of negotiation-related code paths. Full LLVM-IR slicing is a design target not yet implemented. Table caption explicitly states 'estimated from codebase sizes'.

claims.claim: The bounded-exhaustive validator dynamically checks axioms P1-P4 against the extracted LTS, calibrates adversary bounds by incrementally increasing k, and profiles SMT encoding/solving time across a (k, n) grid.

evidence.description: Bounded exhaustive validator implementation (negsyn-eval::bounded_exhaustive_validator) performing axiom validation, bound calibration, and SMT performance profiling.

claims.claim: Protocol bisimilarity is defined as equivalence of negotiation outcome, handshake phase, and observable behavior under all adversary actions, following standard bisimulation theory (Milner 1989, Kanellakis & Smolka 1990).

evidence.description: Definition 1 in the paper formalizes protocol bisimilarity as: same negotiation outcome, same handshake phase, and equivalent observable behaviors under all adversary actions. This follows standard bisimulation theory.
references: Milner, R. 'Communication and Concurrency.' Prentice Hall, 1989.
Kanellakis, P.C. and Smolka, S.A. 'CCS Expressions, Finite State Processes, and Three Problems of Equivalence.' Information and Computation, 1990.
